entries.title: Jacobian Ratio Quality
entries.content: The Jacobian ratio measures element distortion. A ratio close to 1.0 indicates a well-shaped element. Values below 0.1 indicate severe distortion and may cause solver failures. For quadrilateral elements, maintain Jacobian > 0.3 for reliable results.
examples: Negative Jacobian in hole boundary after large delta_R

entries.title: Element Aspect Ratio
entries.content: Aspect ratio is the ratio of longest to shortest element edge. Keep aspect ratio below 10:1 for accuracy. High aspect ratios (>50:1) can cause numerical issues and poor stress predictions, especially near boundaries.

entries.title: Mesh Transition Zones
entries.content: When mesh density changes, use gradual transitions. Element size should not change by more than 2:1 between adjacent elements. Abrupt transitions cause stress concentrations and accuracy loss.
examples: Transition from fine hole boundary to coarse far field

entries.title: Hole Boundary Mesh Density
entries.content: Stress concentrations at holes require fine meshes. Use at least 8 elements around a circular hole for adequate stress resolution. For accuracy in stress concentration factors, use 16+ elements.
examples: Underestimated stress due to coarse hole mesh

entries.title: Morphing Displacement Limits
entries.content: Mesh morphing should not displace nodes more than 50% of the smallest adjacent element edge length. Larger displacements risk element inversion. For hole radius changes, limit delta_R to maintain positive Jacobian.
examples: Element collapse at delta_R > 1.5 for initial R=2.5

entries.title: Linear Elastic Properties
entries.content: For linear elastic materials, Young's modulus E must be positive and Poisson's ratio nu must satisfy 0 <= nu < 0.5. Steel: E~200 GPa, nu~0.3. Aluminum: E~70 GPa, nu~0.33.

entries.title: Material Unit Consistency
entries.content: Ensure consistent units: SI (Pa, m, kg) or mm-based (MPa, mm, tonne). Mixed units cause orders-of-magnitude errors. Check E, density, and geometry units match.
examples: E in Pa with geometry in mm gives wrong stresses

entries.title: Newton-Raphson Convergence
entries.content: Abaqus uses Newton-Raphson iteration. Convergence requires both residual force and displacement corrections to be small. Non-convergence often indicates excessive loading or mesh issues.
examples: Too many iterations in nonlinear analysis

entries.title: Increment Cutback Handling
entries.content: When solver cuts back increment size, it indicates difficulty converging. Multiple cutbacks suggest model problems. Check boundary conditions, contact, and mesh quality when cutbacks occur.

entries.title: Step Size Settings
entries.content: Use initial increment 0.1-0.2 for linear problems. For nonlinear: start with 0.01. Min increment ~1e-6, max increment 0.2. Smaller initial increments help establish equilibrium.
examples: Initial=1.0 causes immediate divergence in nonlinear

entries.title: Boundary Condition Consistency
entries.content: All DOFs must be properly constrained to prevent rigid body motion. For 2D plane stress: fix at least 3 DOFs (2 translations + 1 rotation equivalent). Over-constraint causes stress locking.

entries.title: Symmetry Boundary Conditions
entries.content: For symmetric models, apply symmetry BC: fix normal displacement, free tangential. Reduces model size by 2x (half-symmetry) or 4x (quarter). Must verify symmetry in geometry and loading.
examples: Quarter model of plate with central hole

entries.title: Load Application Points
entries.content: Avoid point loads - they cause singularities. Distribute loads over areas or edges. Use coupling constraints to distribute loads realistically.
examples: Infinite stress at point load location

entries.title: CPS4 vs CPS8 Elements
entries.content: CPS4: 4-node bilinear, efficient but may lock in bending. CPS8: 8-node quadratic, more accurate for stress gradients but computationally expensive. Use CPS8 near holes and stress concentrations.

entries.title: Reduced Integration Warning
entries.content: Reduced integration (CPS4R) is faster but can exhibit hourglassing (zero-energy modes). Use hourglass control or full integration for better accuracy.
examples: Hourglass modes in uniform stress field

entries.title: Mesh Independence Study
entries.content: Always perform mesh convergence study. Refine mesh until results change by <5% with further refinement. Focus refinement on high gradient regions.
examples: Stress changes 20% from coarse to fine mesh

entries.title: Result Verification
entries.content: Verify results against analytical solutions when available. For plate with hole: SCF ~3 for infinite plate. Check equilibrium: sum of reaction forces = applied loads.

entries.title: Incremental Morphing Strategy
entries.content: For large geometry changes, use incremental morphing. Start with small delta_R (~10% of radius) and gradually increase. Validate mesh quality at each step.

entries.title: Negative Jacobian Error
entries.content: Error: ELEMENT X HAS NEGATIVE JACOBIAN. Cause: element is inverted (nodes crossed over). Fix: reduce morphing displacement, refine mesh, or adjust anchor positions.

entries.title: Too Many Attempts Error
entries.content: Error: TOO MANY ATTEMPTS MADE FOR THIS INCREMENT. Cause: solver cannot find equilibrium. Fix: reduce step size, check BCs, improve mesh, or add stabilization.
examples: Contact problem with insufficient mesh density

entries.title: Excessive Distortion Warning
entries.content: Warning: EXCESSIVE DISTORTION AT N INTEGRATION POINTS. Elements are severely deformed. May still complete but results unreliable. Check deformed shape for element collapse.

entries.title: Numerical Singularity
entries.content: Error: NUMERICAL SINGULARITY. Stiffness matrix is singular. Causes: insufficient constraints, disconnected regions, or zero stiffness elements. Check BCs and mesh connectivity.

entries.title: IDW Morphing Parameters
entries.content: IDW (Inverse Distance Weighting) morphing: p parameter controls influence decay. p=2 (default) works for most cases. Higher p localizes deformation. For transition zones, p=2.5 provides smoother transitions.

entries.title: Anchor Node Selection
entries.content: Anchor nodes should be far from moving boundaries. Minimum distance: 2-3x the morphing displacement. Anchors too close to moving region cause mesh collapse.

entries.title: Safe delta_R Range
entries.content: For hole expansion with initial radius R0, typical safe range is delta_R in [-0.5*R0, 0.8*R0]. Larger expansions require anchor reassignment or remeshing. Contractions limited by element collapse.
